vulnerability.reason: Generated describes system inability to enforce canonicality, while reference describes user inability to distinguish encodings; related but different sensitivity condition.
vulnerability.reference: Users cannot distinguish between canonical and noncanonical encodings in generated text.
vulnerability.generated: Training-free canonicalization strategies cannot enforce canonical token encodings, leaving probability distributions susceptible to distortion.

impact.reason: Reference impact is subtle factual errors in educational materials; generated impact is lower-quality text for users, missing both affected party and damage type.
impact.reference: Educational materials contain subtle factual errors from encoding-induced probability shifts.
impact.generated: Users encounter lower-quality, less reliable text generation as probability mass shifts to implausible outputs.

dose_response.reason: Generated arc depicts strategy failure to enforce encodings, whereas reference describes probability shifts directly exposing users to uncontrolled shifts; different causal mechanism.
dose_response.reference: Tokenization mismatch distorts token probabilities, which in training-free canonicalization exposes users to uncontrolled probability shifts, leading to subtle factual errors in educational materials and polluted public discourse.
dose_response.generated: Tokenization mismatch between training and inference, through users of training-free text generation pipelines, triggers the inability of canonicalization strategies to enforce canonical encodings and prevent probability distribution distortion, leading to lower-quality text generation as probability mass shifts to implausible outputs.
